data.title: Please stop asking me to be your best man - I've spent $4,400 on one wedding and can't afford it anymore
data.description:
By Weston Blasi

Guilt over how good a friend I am is colliding with the financial reality that this wedding will cost me thousands of dollars

The average cost to attend a local wedding is $610, and going to an out-of-town wedding now runs $1,680 on average, according to the Knot. But if you're a groomsman at a fancier wedding, the charges don't stop there - especially when the groom is a close friend.

If I don't come to your wedding, it is not my fault, it's yours.

Being asked to be a groomsman for one of your closest friends should be a joy - the precursor to an unforgettable weekend that you'll remember forever. But increasingly for me, those feelings of joy get crushed by a wave of credit-card bills for a wedding that wasn't even mine.

Much has been made of the rising costs of weddings - the average wedding is now $33,000. Being invited to a wedding has become a major expense, too. The average cost to attend a local wedding is $610, while going to an out-of-town wedding now runs $1,680 on average, according to the Knot, a wedding e-commerce site.

But if you're a groomsman at a fancier wedding, the charges don't stop there - especially when the groom is a close friend.

Expenses can include the cost to rent a tuxedo and the cost of attending the bachelor party - which can be pricey if you rent a ski lodge for a weekend and buy food and drinks, as I did before my childhood best friend's upcoming wedding. Then there's the cost of getting a more meaningful gift due to the closeness of our relationship.

In this case, the groom is not only a childhood friend, but a best friend that has remained close into adulthood, which can be rare. He's somebody I golf with and call on the phone to see how he's doing. Most importantly, we count on each other for advice when making big life choices.

In other words, this is a relationship I want to maintain throughout my life. But as the expenses rise, guilt over how good a friend I am collides with the financial reality that this wedding could cost me thousands of dollars. That sets up some uncomfortable choices.

I've now spent nearly $4,400 to be a part of my childhood best friend's upcoming wedding in Georgia. Here's a breakdown:

As you can see above, I've compiled all wedding-related events, not just the wedding itself. Some of them may be more expensive than ones you've been to. But when the groom is a good friend and wants to have a pricey bachelor party at a ski resort, or a wedding on an island that you need to take a boat to, can I really say no?

There's evidence that many wedding invitees wish they could do just that. Online wedding-registry platform Zola's internal research showed that 90% of couples wish it was more socially acceptable to decline an invite to a prewedding event. And 75% of them wished it was more acceptable to decline an invitation to be in a person's wedding party.

Rising costs are fueling an increase in people declining wedding invitations altogether: 1 in 3 Americans said they decided not to attend a wedding in 2024 over cost concerns, up from 1 in 5 who said the same in 2016.

Posts with thousands of comments on the social-media site Reddit (RDDT) show that many are less than overjoyed about being in a wedding party.

"I'd rather use my PTO to go vacation somewhere quiet rather than have to go to all the extra events associated with the wedding," wrote one poster, who wanted to know if he was a jerk for declining to be a groomsman in a close friend's wedding.

"The problem is I'm not doing well financially and I'd need to use a month's salary just for a both way plane ticket," wrote another user, who described the painful decision to not serve as best man for a childhood friend.

The multiday celebration takes an extra financial toll

Another financial issue is that weddings seem to be getting longer - not the length of the ceremony itself, but the number of days where your time and presence are needed. Some weddings, including the one I described above, now have events on Sunday and Thursday, in addition to the traditional Friday-Saturday lineup of rehearsal dinner, ceremony and subsequent party.

This Georgia wedding will include an 18-hole golf outing with the groomsmen on the Thursday before the ceremony, and a "farewell brunch" on Sunday (which, as far as I know, is free - so I dodged another expense there).

Golf is not only expensive to play ($270 for a round), but the outing extends the trip another day as well, which increases lodging costs. (Still, it is one of our favorite things to do.)

Four days of time is a lot to ask of a wedding guest. Extra activities can make it increasingly costly if guests have babysitting or pet-sitting expenses associated with the trip - not to mention added time off from work, which wasn't included in this calculation.

How to control the cost of being in a wedding party

Of course, there are always ways to save money on wedding festivities. For instance, I'll try to carpool to and from airports, split my hotel room with a friend, and see if my mom can cut my hair that week instead of my expensive New York City barber.

It also helps to know exactly how much you can reasonably spend before you finalize your plans.

"Before you start booking travel or buying gifts, decide how much you're comfortable spending for this wedding, and try to stick to it," Emily Forrest, head of communications at Zola, told MarketWatch. "That number will look different for everyone depending on finances, where the wedding is and how close you are to the couple. And that's OK - an invitation isn't a bill."

And if you are invited to a wedding, whether as a guest or a groomsman, and simply can't afford to go, then don't go. The bride or groom will understand if you don't have the money - especially if they're a true friend.

"At the end of the day, couples invite you to their wedding because they love you - not because of how much you spend," said Lauren Kay, executive editor at the Knot. "Thoughtful, honest decisions always matter more than financial overextension, and we always encourage guests to celebrate within their means."

Unfortunately for my wallet, my guilt and fear of missing out on the fun won't let me miss this wedding - I'd probably spend $10,000 to fly to New Zealand and take the 14-day "Lord of the Rings" location tour if that was part of the wedding festivities. But that doesn't mean I can't complain about the costs.

And if you think the cost of this wedding is bad, just know that I've got four more to go to this year.

If you're just starting out on your money or career journey and have questions about how to navigate your finances, we want to hear from you. Write to Dollar Signs, MarketWatch's new advice column, at dollarsigns@marketwatch.com.

-Weston Blasi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  08-23-25 0800ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: I'm 32 and only have $8,000 saved in my 401(k) so far. How do I get more aggressive?
data.description:
By Beth Pinsker

The answer doesn't have anything to do with which investments you choose.

Got a question about investing, how it fits into your overall financial plan and what strategies can help you make the most out of your money? You can write to me at beth.pinsker@marketwatch.com. Please put "Fix My Portfolio" in the subject line.

Dear Fix My Portfolio,

I'm 32 and have an 11-year-old daughter. I work in banking and I would like to be more aggressive with my 401(k). I only have $8,000 now. I have made two withdrawals over the past years, which explains the lower balance. I also have two savings accounts, with a little over $1,200 deposited. I am now taking investing seriously, attempting to get out of the rat race. Can you advise me on what next steps I should focus on?

Ready to Rock

Dear Ready,

It might not seem like it, but you're on a good path right now. You started a retirement plan and you have an emergency fund. Those two things will help your future in enormous ways that are hard to see right now.

If you want to get more aggressive about your 401(k), the way to think about it is not about what funds you invest in. Just invest in something, and time will take care of the rest. Most people in your position pick a single target-date mutual fund offered in their plan and just stick with it. That's a fine option if you don't want to fuss too much with things. Your presumed retirement date is far in the future, probably another 35 years, so think about a fund in the 2060 range. The allocation right now will be mostly stocks, and will get more conservative as you age. If you want to be more selective about it, split your contributions in some other ratio among the index-fund choices available in your company's plan.

Your returns over the years will likely not vary too much whichever way you go. Most of what controls investment returns is beyond your control - interest-rate policies, macroeconomic conditions and wild cards like the COVID-19 pandemic. Fees matter, and target-date funds typically charge more a year than index funds, but you have to factor in ease of use, too.

The thing that will really move the needle is saving more. Along with that goes the caveat of not taking money out while you're trying to build savings. It seems like you've already learned that lesson, though, because you have built up an emergency fund.

Saving more is hard, and sometimes the only way to do it is to earn more. There's only so much you can do to tighten a budget. When you have a young child, there are necessary expenses and unexpected costs all the time. You really can't get around it.

What that means for you in your early 30s is to constantly be thinking about what you can do to make more money. If you wait at your job for steady raises, it will be a slow but sure route. Banking is generally stable employment, and you can work your way up. But maybe you want more than that.

The first stop is your company's human-resources department. See what educational training they offer as part of your compensation. You might be able to get a Series 7 license and step up to be an investment adviser at your financial firm. You might even be eligible to study to become a certified financial planner. For both of these, your work in banking will count toward the experience requirement, and it will be up to you to complete the coursework and pass the tests.

This is what I did to push my own career forward - although via journalism, not banking. The work is hard, and sometimes a little tedious. My own kids were about the same age as yours when I was studying for the CFP exam; I would do my math homework on the couch while they did theirs. Eventually, I passed the test (although I can't tell you it was easy). My youngest still does her math homework on the couch - but I have no clue what she's talking about anymore with her AP Calculus.

I've since moved on to other side hustles, which is your other option. You might think you've got enough on your plate already and can't take on one more thing, but this is what being aggressive about retirement savings is really about. You can't just magically make a significant amount of money in the stock market - you have to put money in there in the first place. Your $8,000 is a good start, but you have to keep adding to it to turn it into a real fortune.

Think about the math this way: If $8,000 grows at a very good average rate of 10% a year for 35 years until you retire, you'll have about $225,000 saved up. If you add $1,000 to that every year, you'd have nearly $500,000. If you double that and add $2,000 a year, you'd have more than $750,000 - and at that point, you'd be able to sustain making less than 10% a year, which wouldn't be in your control. You might have some negative years in that time frame that could drag you down.

A side hustle or a higher-paying job would allow you to make those contributions - $2,000 split among 26 paychecks is just $77 each period-and still have money for emergencies. That would stop you from withdrawing from your retirement savings and allow you to afford the things you need for your child. Another mental trick to consider is increasing your contribution rate by 1% each year, until you reach about 15% or the maximum contribution allowed a year.

I have high hopes for you. I think you can take that $8,000 and double it in no time with some hard work, and then keep letting the magic of compound interest work for you. Good luck!

You can also join the Retirement conversation in our Facebook community: Retire Better with MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Beth Pinsker

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  08-23-25 0800ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Weekend Payday Loans for Bad Credit: Radcred Introduces Saturday and Sunday Payday Loans For Bad Credit Guaranteed Approval For US Borrowers
data.description:
Glendale, CA, Aug. 23, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) --  RadCred, a U.S.-based AI loan matching platform, has introduced a new solution for borrowers in need of urgent funds with the launch of weekend payday loans for bad credit. Through its proprietary AI system, RadCred now enables applicants to access Saturday and Sunday payday loans, ensuring financial relief even when traditional banks are closed. The platform matches borrowers with state-licensed lenders using real-time AI evaluation, offering instant approval-style prequalification without hard credit checks. Loan terms, APRs, and repayment structures are disclosed upfront, with funds often deposited the same day. 

Focusing on transparency and accessibility, RadCred ensures borrowers with poor or limited credit histories can secure reliable funding on weekends in a fast and compliant manner.

Why Weekend Payday Loans Are Surging in Popularity?

According to industry search data, queries for weekend payday loans, Saturday payday loans, and Sunday payday loans have significantly increased over the past two years. The rise reflects the financial reality for millions of Americans: urgent bills, medical emergencies, or car repairs rarely wait until Monday.

For borrowers with poor credit histories, the challenge is even more severe. Traditional banks often deny applications due to low FICO scores, leaving many to search online for alternatives such as no credit check loans or bad credit loans guaranteed approval. RadCred recognized this unmet demand and introduced AI-driven weekend payday loans, designed to ensure that U.S. borrowers can access funds quickly, securely, and responsibly.

What Are Weekend Payday Loans?

Weekend payday loans are short-term, high-interest loans designed to provide quick cash access when traditional banks are closed. These emergency loans typically range from $100 to $1,000 (or more, depending on credit) and are intended to cover urgent expenses until your next paycheck arrives. Key things borrowers should know:

  • Repayment terms are usually 2-4 weeks, with full payment due on your next payday
  • Quick approval process with minimal documentation, often requiring proof of income and a bank account
  • Same-day funding may be available through direct deposit or cash pickup locations
  • State regulations vary significantly, with some states prohibiting payday loans entirely

Saturday and Sunday payday loans could be great to manage true emergencies, especially if you can get fast disbursement. That’s where RadCred truly shines.

How RadCred’s Weekend Payday Loans Work?

Borrowers can secure weekend payday loans for bad credit through RadCred’s AI-powered platform in just four steps:

  1. Apply Online Anytime - Applications can be submitted 24/7 through RadCred’s secure digital portal. Unlike traditional banks that close on weekends, RadCred offers access to Saturday payday loans and Sunday payday loans, making it easier for borrowers to request funds whenever emergencies strike.

  2. AI-Powered Prequalification - The platform performs a soft credit check that does not affect FICO scores. This process allows applicants to explore no credit check loans and bad credit loans guaranteed approval-style offers without risking their credit history.

  3. Instant Loan Matching - RadCred’s proprietary AI evaluates more than 100 data points, including income stability, spending habits, repayment capacity, and banking activity. After analyzing these factors, the system instantly connects borrowers with lenders offering personal loans for bad credit, emergency loans, or online payday loans tailored to their needs.

  4. Same-Day or Weekend Funding - Once an offer is accepted, funds are typically deposited directly into the borrower’s account on the same day. With RadCred’s network of lenders, many applicants receive fast funding loans even on weekends, providing crucial financial relief when it’s needed most.

Through this streamlined process, RadCred ensures that weekend payday loans for bad credit with instant approval are not only accessible but also transparent, secure, and compliant with state lending regulations.

Finding the Best Weekend Payday Loans for Bad Credit With RadCred

RadCred helps borrowers with poor or limited credit access safe and reliable weekend payday loans without hidden costs or predatory practices. The platform ensures:

  • Flexible Loan Amounts: Borrowers can access weekend payday loans from $200 or larger personal loans for bad credit up to $35,000, depending on their financial needs.

  • Transparent Terms: Every loan offer clearly discloses APR, repayment schedules, origination fees, late fees, and rollover terms, eliminating surprises.

  • Repayment Options: Borrowers can compare weekly, biweekly, or monthly repayment structures, making bad credit loans guaranteed approval-style offers more manageable.

  • Same-Day & Weekend Funding: RadCred connects borrowers with lenders who provide Saturday payday loans and Sunday payday loans, ensuring fast relief during emergencies.

  • Licensed Lender Network: All offers come from state-regulated lenders, protecting consumers applying for no credit check loans and emergency loans for bad credit.

By combining transparency, flexibility, and speed, RadCred ensures U.S. borrowers can secure weekend payday loans for bad credit that are both compliant and reliable.

Benefits of Choosing RadCred for Weekend Payday Loans

Borrowers gain several advantages when applying for Saturday payday loans or Sunday payday loans through RadCred’s AI-driven platform:

  • 24/7 Access: Loan applications processed instantly on weekends and holidays.

  • Guaranteed Approval-Style Prequalification: High approval likelihood for borrowers with poor credit histories.

  • Transparency: APRs, repayment terms, and fees disclosed upfront.

  • Credit Score Protection: Soft inquiries protect FICO scores during prequalification.

  • Licensed Lenders Only: All offers come from compliant, state-regulated providers.

  • Flexible Options: Choose from payday loans online, installment loans, emergency loans for bad credit, or personal loans for bad credit.

  • AI-Powered Accuracy: RadCred’s system evaluates borrower profiles beyond credit scores, including income stability and banking patterns, to increase fair access.

Weekend Payday Loans vs. Traditional Payday Loans

Traditional payday loans are often associated with steep fees, rigid repayment schedules, and limited availability. Borrowers typically have to repay the full amount plus interest within a short period, usually two weeks, which can create a cycle of dependency. Many payday lenders also impose rollover fees, late penalties, and high APRs that make repayment difficult. 

Since most banks and lenders do not process applications during weekends, borrowers facing emergencies on Saturdays or Sundays are left with no viable options. For individuals with poor or limited credit histories, these obstacles make traditional payday loans both restrictive and financially risky.

Weekend payday loans through RadCred, however, provide a more flexible and transparent alternative. Using AI-powered matching, RadCred connects borrowers with licensed lenders that offer Saturday payday loans, Sunday payday loans, and bad credit loans with guaranteed approval-style prequalification. 

Loan amounts range from $200 to $35,000 with repayment terms extending from weeks to 24 months, offering significantly more choice than traditional payday loans. RadCred’s platform ensures borrowers receive upfront disclosures on APR, origination fees, repayment structures, and funding speed, reducing the likelihood of hidden costs. 

With 24/7 online access and soft credit checks that protect FICO scores, RadCred delivers a compliant, consumer-focused model that makes weekend payday loans for bad credit accessible, safe, and reliable.

Conclusion

With the launch of its Saturday payday loans and Sunday payday loans for bad credit, RadCred is redefining access to short-term lending in the U.S. The platform’s AI-powered system makes it possible for borrowers to secure weekend payday loans with no credit check, receive instant approval-style prequalification, and review offers with full transparency. 

Unlike traditional payday lenders, RadCred prioritizes compliance, consumer protection, and fair access for borrowers with poor or limited credit histories. By combining technology with trusted lender partnerships, RadCred ensures faster decisions, same-day or weekend funding, and clear repayment structures. As demand for bad credit loans guaranteed approval continues to grow in 2025, RadCred has earned a reputation as a reliable, responsible, and technology-driven solution.

FAQ

Q1. Can I really get payday loans on Saturday or Sunday with RadCred?
Yes. RadCred’s AI platform allows borrowers to apply for Saturday payday loans and Sunday payday loans, with funding possible even on weekends depending on lender and bank processing.

Q2. Will applying for weekend payday loans affect my credit score?
No. RadCred uses a soft credit check during prequalification, meaning your FICO score is not impacted when exploring no credit check loans or bad credit loans guaranteed approval-style offers.

Q3. How much can I borrow with RadCred weekend payday loans?
Loan amounts typically range from $200 to $35,000, covering short-term needs like emergency loans for bad credit as well as larger personal loans for bad credit.

Q4. Are RadCred weekend payday loans safe and legal?
Yes. RadCred only partners with licensed and state-regulated lenders, ensuring that all weekend payday loans for bad credit comply with lending laws and consumer protection standards.

Q5. How fast can I receive funding through RadCred’s weekend payday loans?
Most borrowers receive same-day or next-day deposits. RadCred’s AI system matches you instantly with lenders that prioritize fast funding loans, even on weekends.

About RadCred

RadCred is a U.S.-based AI loan-matching platform that connects borrowers with licensed payday, personal, and installment lenders. Specializing in weekend payday loans, no credit check loans, bad credit loans guaranteed approval, and emergency loans, RadCred delivers instant approvals, same-day or weekend funding, and transparent terms. Its mission is to empower borrowers with safe, flexible, and compliant financial solutions across all 50 states.

Disclaimer

RadCred is a platform offering short-term consumer loans where permitted by law. All loans are subject to state lending regulations and the lender’s internal approval criteria, including income verification and ability to repay. Loan approval, funding timelines, loan amounts, and APRs vary by applicant qualifications, lender requirements, and state guidelines. "Guaranteed approval" refers to RadCred's AI-powered prequalification system, which matches borrowers to high-likelihood offers; however, final approval remains subject to lender verification and no loan is guaranteed for every borrower. Weekend deposits may depend on bank processing hours.

data.title: BRBR CLASS REMINDER: Suffer Losses on Your BellRing Brands, Inc. Investment? Contact BFA Law about the Securities Fraud Investigation (NYSE:BRBR)
data.description:
NEW YORK, Aug. 23, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Leading securities law firm Bleichmar Fonti & Auld LLP announces an investigation into BellRing Brands, Inc. (NYSE: BRBR) for potential violations of the federal securities laws.

If you invested in BellRing, you are encouraged to obtain additional information by visiting: https://www.bfalaw.com/cases-investigations/bellring-brands-inc-class-action-lawsuit.

Why is BellRing Being Investigated?

BellRing Brands operates in the convenient nutrition category. The Company’s primary brands include Premier Protein and Dymatize, which offer ready-to-drink (“RTD”) protein shakes and powders. During the relevant period, the Company stated that Premier Protein “hit an all-time high in household penetration” and that “demand remains strong.” The Company also stated that its growth was “strong in all channels,” driven by “distribution expansion, accelerating velocities and incremental promotional activity.”

In truth, the Company’s sales growth during the relevant period may have been driven by temporary trade inventory loading at several key retailers, not sustainable end-consumer demand.

The Stock Declines as the Truth Is Revealed

On May 5, 2025, after market hours, BellRing revealed that starting in Q2 2023, “several key retailers lowered their weeks of supply on hand,” which would create a headwind to Q3 2025 growth. The Company also announced it was expanding promotions to boost sales and “offset [] third quarter reductions in retailer trade inventory levels.” On this news, the price of BellRing stock fell $13.96 per share, or more than 18%, from $77.34 per share on May 5, 2025, to $63.38 per share on May 6, 2025.

Then, on August 4, 2025, after market hours, BellRing announced disappointing quarterly consumption of Premier Protein RTD Shakes, which had been expected to outpace shipments by a wider margin given previously announced retailer destocking, but instead came “more in line” with shipments. On this news, the price of BellRing Brands stock fell $17.46 per share, or nearly 33%, from $53.64 per share on August 4, 2025, to $36.18 per share on August 5, 2025.

Click here for more information: https://www.bfalaw.com/cases-investigations/bellring-brands-inc-class-action-lawsuit.

What Can You Do?

If you invested in BellRing you may have legal options and are encouraged to submit your information to the firm.

All representation is on a contingency fee basis, there is no cost to you. Shareholders are not responsible for any court costs or expenses of litigation. The firm will seek court approval for any potential fees and expenses.

Submit your information by visiting:

https://www.bfalaw.com/cases-investigations/bellring-brands-inc-class-action-lawsuit

Or contact:
Ross Shikowitz
ross@bfalaw.com
212.789.3619

Why Bleichmar Fonti & Auld LLP?

BFA is a leading international law firm representing plaintiffs in securities class actions and shareholder litigation. It has been named a top plaintiff law firm by Chambers USA, The Legal 500, and ISS SCAS, and its attorneys have been named “Elite Trial Lawyers” by the National Law Journal, among the top “500 Leading Plaintiff Financial Lawyers” by Lawdragon, “Titans of the Plaintiffs’ Bar” by Law360 and “SuperLawyers” by Thomson Reuters. Among its recent notable successes, BFA recovered over $900 million in value from Tesla, Inc.’s Board of Directors, as well as $420 million from Teva Pharmaceutical Ind. Ltd.

For more information about BFA and its attorneys, please visit https://www.bfalaw.com.

https://www.bfalaw.com/cases-investigations/bellring-brands-inc-class-action-lawsuit

Attorney advertising. Past results do not guarantee future outcomes.

data.title: CHTR CLASS REMINDER: Suffer Losses on Your Charter Communications, Inc. Investment? Contact BFA Law before the October 14 Court Deadline (NASDAQ:CHTR)
data.description:
NEW YORK, Aug. 23, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Leading securities law firm Bleichmar Fonti & Auld LLP announces that a lawsuit has been filed against Charter Communications, Inc. (NASDAQ: CHTR) and certain of the Company’s senior executives for potential violations of the federal securities laws.

If you invested in Charter, you are encouraged to obtain additional information by visiting: https://www.bfalaw.com/cases/charter-communications-inc-class-action-lawsuit.

Investors have until October 14, 2025, to ask the Court to be appointed to lead the case. The complaint asserts claims under Sections 10(b) and 20(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 on behalf of investors in Charter securities. The case is pending in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York and is captioned Sandoval v. Charter Communications, Inc., No. 1:25-cv-06747.

Why Was Charter Sued Under the Federal Securities Laws?

Charter is a leading broadband, or high-speed internet, connectivity company and cable operator. Charter participated in the FCC’s Affordable Connectivity Program (“ACP”), which provided funding to Charter in exchange for subsidizing high-speed internet plans for low-income households. In June 2024, lack of federal funding caused the ACP to end, which led to customer declines at Charter.

During the relevant period, Charter told investors that the Company was executing a plan to minimize and move beyond risks that the end of the ACP had on customer declines and earnings. The Company stated that it had “managed the end of the affordable connectivity program successfully” and that “[t]he impact of the elimination of the ACP is now behind us.” As alleged, in truth, the impact from the ACP’s elimination was not behind Charter as the Company continued to experience internet customer and revenue declines from the program’s end.

The Stock Declines as the Truth Is Revealed

On July 25, 2025, Charter announced its second quarter 2025 financial results. The Company reported that total internet customers decreased by 117,000 during the quarter, which included approximately 50,000 disconnects related to the end of the ACP, nearly double from the prior quarter. On this news, the price of Charter stock declined $70.25 per share, or 18.4%, from a closing price of $380.00 per share on July 24, 2025, to $309.75 per share on July 25, 2025.

Click here for more information: https://www.bfalaw.com/cases/charter-communications-inc-class-action-lawsuit.

What Can You Do?

If you invested in Charter you may have legal options and are encouraged to submit your information to the firm.

All representation is on a contingency fee basis, there is no cost to you. Shareholders are not responsible for any court costs or expenses of litigation. The firm will seek court approval for any potential fees and expenses.

Submit your information by visiting:

https://www.bfalaw.com/cases/charter-communications-inc-class-action-lawsuit

Or contact:
Ross Shikowitz
ross@bfalaw.com

Among its recent notable successes, BFA recovered over $900 million in value from Tesla, Inc.’s Board of Directors, as well as $420 million from Teva Pharmaceutical Ind. Ltd.

data.title: 'I've struggled in every way': My brother passed away and I'm $28,000 in debt. How do I get out of this mess?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'Has my grief affected my situation? Looking back, I think it did.'

"I recently started working from home, and that's let me cut back on some gas and food costs." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

I'm really struggling with my current debts - they're eating up so much of my monthly income. I don't qualify for a consolidation loan right now, and although one financial-services company offered to "restructure" my debt, it feels predatory. I can't risk damaging my credit score further - it's currently around 650. I've been using the "snowball method" to pay down my debt, but as my other expenses keep rising, it's getting harder to keep up.

I feel like I'm drowning, and I'm wondering if there are any other options I might be missing. Please, no judgment. I had no credit-card debt until about four years ago. My husband went back to school and took a pay cut - a long-term blessing, but a short-term financial strain. Then my brother passed away two years ago, and since then I've struggled in every way - mentally, physically and financially.

Related: I'm a widow living off Social Security. I have $200K in a brokerage account, so why am I ignored?

A year of grieving

Has my grief affected my situation? Looking back, I think it did. I went through a year or so where I didn't have the energy to figure things out, so sometimes I just did what was easiest and that sometimes meant using a credit card. I did what I had to do at the time to make it mentally and emotionally and still be a good mother. I've learned a lot about grief and what all it affects, and I hope to help other people through it whenever I can.

We eat out maybe once a month. We cook at home and make that special as best we can. I recently started working from home, and that's let me cut back on gas and food costs. But switching jobs caused me to go for a month with no pay, and it's been hard to get back on track. My debts total $28,400 and the monthly payments are $948, split between four companies ($151, $411, $249 and $137). One loan has $1,500 left.

I'm embarrassed and frustrated that I've let things get this out of hand.

Anonymous Wife & Mother

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Don't miss: 'I can only rely on myself': How do I protect myself from my parents' nursing-home bills?

Be kind to yourself. That will make the months ahead easier and allow you to live your life without shame or guilt or anger over the past.

Dear Anonymous,

You're not drowning.

The slow, steady drip of payments you are making will fill that bucket. You need to take care of your mental health as much as your physical health and be kind to yourself while you pay these debts off. That can be one of your takeaways when you finally get back in the black. Because it will happen: It's just a question of when, and until then it's an exercise in fiscal discipline and patience. Everyone has something they're dealing with, even if it's not credit-card debt or personal loans.

Opinion is divided on whether it's best to focus on paying off debts with the highest interest rate (the avalanche method) or the lowest dollar figure (the snowball method). I favor the former, but whatever keeps you motivated is what works. The snowball method is a way to motivate people by chipping away at the number of debts they owe, but paying down the highest rate first makes the most sense to me. If $10,000 of your debt is on a high-interest credit card, I'd put my extra cash toward that.

Don't miss: As a nurse, I've seen people use their power of attorney to take advantage of their parents. Watch out for these warnings.

Seek real-time support

The National Foundation for Credit Counseling is a nonprofit organization that can help you put together a budget and a realistic plan to pay off your debt. A credit union, if you're a member of one, can help you with that too. Another nonprofit, American Consumer Credit Counseling, is also there to help people who are in your position. And 12-step support groups, including Debtors Anonymous, provide a safe space to talk about your background and your emotional life.

Grief counselors and financial advisers often recommend that you don't make any major financial decisions during the first year after losing a loved one. There's wisdom in that advice: Someone might decide to give up their job and sell their house - anything to help them escape their feelings or even reality. But less is written about what you touched upon: There are still lots of small financial decisions that must be made, like whether to put purchases on a credit card or take out personal loans.

Your debt is high enough that you should make it your top financial priority, and that is best done in a strategic manner, rather than ad hoc. Work out a monthly budget of your income and expenditures, including utilities, property taxes, home insurance, mortgage payments, groceries and gas, and stick to it. If saving $100 or $200 a month on eating out helps you pay off one of your debts, plan an extra-special night cooking at home instead - choose a $15 bottle of wine or play a family card game, for example.

Embrace your mistakes - and future

You also need to do some soul-searching. That will help you get clarity about your past and improve your mental health. Why did you put so many purchases on plastic? How did it make you feel? Were those feelings temporary? What purpose, if any, does beating yourself up after the fact solve? If we face up to our sometimes chaotic and complex emotional life, we can break that cycle (cutting up the credit cards also helps). It's not about blame. It's not about shame. It's about self-examination and forgiveness.

My final message is to be kind to yourself. That will make the months ahead easier and allow you to live your life without shame or guilt or anger over the past. You are not the past. You are the present, and you are passing through that place of grief and personal debt as if you are on a train. You will pull into a new station free of these financial and emotional burdens, and you will look back on this time of your life and not feel frustrated or embarrassed. Instead, you will and should feel proud.

Don't miss: I'm single, 75, and living with kidney disease and a pacemaker. I've no long-term care insurance. Will I be OK?

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

My friend's father gifted her Tesla and Google stock. They could be worth millions. Will they be split 50/50 in her divorce?

'I've been kept in poverty': Do I take a $70K job at 74 and lose my Section 8 housing allowance?

My aunt barely spends any money. Will we receive the contents of her safe-deposit box when she dies?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

-Quentin Fottrell

data.title: Bitcoin Swift Announces Early Launch on August 30 as Stage 6 Presale Gains Momentum
data.description:
LUXEMBOURG, Aug. 23, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Bitcoin Swift (BTC3) has announced that its official launch will take place earlier than expected, moving up to August 30, 2025, weeks ahead of its original schedule. This development coincides with the project’s Stage 6 presale, priced at $6, which has already pushed total presale contributions beyond $1 million.

The early launch announcement significantly shortens the timeline between presale participation and live trading, offering investors faster access to liquidity and utility within the Bitcoin Swift ecosystem. Stages 6 and 7 will serve as the final presale phases before BTC3 lists on exchanges.

Stage 6 Presale and Reward Structure

Stage 6 not only offers an entry price of $6 per BTC3 token but also includes 166% APY through programmable Proof-of-Yield (PoY) rewards during the presale phase. In addition, Bitcoin Swift has introduced a tiered bonus program for early buyers:

  • Tier 1 ($100–$1,999): 25% bonus tokens
  • Tier 2 ($2,000–$4,999): 50% bonus tokens
  • Tier 3 ($5,000+): 100% bonus tokens

The program has already attracted significant interest, with Stage 5 distributing more than $110,000 in PoY rewards. With the APY boost and bonus incentives, Stage 6 is seeing even higher engagement.

Example allocations at current presale pricing:

  • A $5,000 Tier 3 purchase secures 1,666 tokens (including the bonus). If BTC3 reaches $20 post-launch, this equals $33,320 in value.
  • A $3,000 Tier 2 purchase secures 1,125 tokens, valued at $22,500 at $20.
  • A $1,500 Tier 1 purchase secures 312 tokens, worth over $6,000 at $20.

Technical Highlights and Ecosystem Features

Bitcoin Swift is being developed as a decentralized financial operating system. Core components include:

  • Adaptive PoY rewards for yield generation
  • AI-powered smart contracts to enhance security and automation
  • Privacy-first decentralized identity features
  • Hybrid on-chain governance for community-driven decision-making

The project also introduces BTC3E, a USD-pegged stablecoin backed by BTC3 at collateral ratios above 150%. AI oracles will monitor solvency, while governance mechanisms adjust stability fees and risk parameters.

Influencer Buzz

Crypto influencers have taken notice, with reviews focusing on BTC3’s tech and potential:

  • Token Galaxy highlighted BTC3’s AI-driven contracts and real DeFi applications.
  • Bull Run Angel praised its hybrid security and governance model, noting how it sets BTC3 apart from typical presales.

Security and Compliance

Bitcoin Swift has completed multiple independent security and compliance checks, including audits by Cyberscope , Solidproof , Spywolf, alongside a verified KYC process. These steps aim to build trust and ensure resilience as the project approaches its mainnet launch.

Countdown to August 30

With less than eight days remaining before launch, investors who enter during Stage 6 and Stage 7 not only secure bonus tokens but also receive real-time PoY rewards before BTC3 goes live. This combination of incentives and accelerated timeline positions Bitcoin Swift as a project moving toward operational readiness ahead of market expectations.

For additional details, visit:
Website: https://bitcoinswift.com
Follow updates on X

Contact:
Luc Schaus
support@bitcoinswift.com

data.title: As a nurse, I've seen people use their power of attorney to take advantage of their parents. Watch out for these warnings.
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'Even if someone has millions of dollars in retirement, that won't keep them from being trapped in the hospital'

"My own stepmother handled her mother's retirement correctly." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

I read your column. You should recommend that people fill out power-of-attorney paperwork. As a nurse, I see the need for this all the time. Wealth does not protect you. Someone with just Social Security in retirement is going to end up in a facility at the bottom of the barrel. Somebody with assets should be enjoying the best there is.

Even if someone has millions of dollars in retirement, that won't keep them from being trapped in the hospital, especially if they're single. What happens is that people often suddenly lose their capacity for decision-making, and it can take up to a year to get a guardian in place via the court system.

Meanwhile, a person who could be enjoying a really high-quality memory-care center - or assisted living - is stuck in a hospital room. Their health deteriorates more quickly, because people are not meant to be staring at the same four walls for a whole year with minimal activities.

Here are some warning signs: If a person is married, their spouse can help, but an older person may have lost their spouse, the one person who could advocate for them. Or both spouses might become incapacitated at the same time. Sometimes the tipping point for one spouse is the stress of their partner's sudden decline.

Don't miss: 'She screamed and raged at me': I have power of attorney for my mother's $3 million estate - but my sister feels sidelined

Trapped in a hospital

I take care of a couple of people who should be able to enjoy assisted living with a high level of freedom but who are stuck in the hospital because they're no longer able to manage their affairs safely. Now they need to have a guardian appointed. It's a horrible situation. It's even worse when they actually have the means to live in a good place.

Another problem is when people who do have a power of attorney, sometimes children, abuse their position grievously. Parents need to choose wisely. Just because somebody's your child doesn't mean they're ethical. (I've seen several cases where the child has neglected their parents, tried to sell their house and take their money.)

Alternatively, a child might try to find the cheapest place instead of the highest-quality place for their father or mother to live so that they can maintain their inheritance. My stepmother handled her mother's retirement correctly. They sold her house and put her money into the highest-quality memory-care center in our area.

She spent the last two years enjoying her life. She socialized with people and was not trapped in a terrible home. As a healthcare provider, it's terrible seeing people leaving the hospital in dire situations, but it's not uncommon. Too many children worry about what's going to be left for them instead of worrying about their parents.

Stepdaughter & Nurse

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Don't miss: 'I can only rely on myself': How do I protect myself from my parents' nursing-home bills?

As a safety measure, set up a dual power of attorney.

Dear Nurse,

Thank you for your perspective as a medical professional. Whenever money is concerned, people will show you their true nature.

Sometimes they will surprise you with their generosity and thoughtfulness. But as you point out, on other occasions they will act in their own best interest - not in the best interest of their aging friend or relative. When you give someone power of attorney, you are putting your life in their hands.

A durable power of attorney allows the trusted individual (the agent) to retain power of attorney even after the person who created the document (the principal) has become incapacitated. A general power of attorney expires when the principal has lost mental capacity and is usually limited to certain legal or financial transactions.

Guardians, meanwhile, are appointed by the courts and have immense powers. Power of attorney is set up by the principal - that is, the person who expects to need care and help with decision-making as they get older. As a safety measure, a person can set up a dual power of attorney - that is, appoint two or more people as agents.

It's very easy for a child to convince their parents, in a vulnerable moment, to sign a POA document. One of the reasons this is especially vulnerable to abuse is because the person who holds that power is not under the jurisdiction of the courts in the same way a guardianship or conservatorship (which have their own problems) would be.

Don't miss: I held power of attorney for my late brother. Can I withdraw money from his bank account to give to his favorite charity?

When an agent steals money

There is a lack of empirical data on elder abuse. "There is currently limited information on the number of guardianship cases involving abuse," according to the Department of Justice. "The U.S. Senate Special Committee on Aging and the U.S. Government Accountability Office have highlighted the problem and cited anecdotal information."

I have, in fact, received many letters over the years, including several in recent months, about people abusing their POA responsibilities. In one case, a son liquidated his mother's 401(k) to pay for her nursing home and kept the rest of the money. His sister wrote to me, understandably distraught as to what to do next.

In another case, which took place during the pandemic, a woman stole her sister's Social Security number and persuaded her sister's husband to sign away his power of attorney when he was in the hospital. I urged the letter writer, whose parents were the victims, to petition the court to revoke her aunt's POA.

In another recent case, a woman wrote to tell me that she believed she had been given her mother's power of attorney, but her mother's adviser had persuaded his client to change beneficiaries on several of her accounts. If her mother was incapacitated, the letter writer would need to petition the courts for guardianship or conservatorship.

Don't miss: 'I have felt invisible, disrespected and heartbroken': My firstborn son wants nothing to do with me. Do I cut him off?

Bad actors fly under the radar

A seemingly trustworthy agent who has been given power of attorney can take many actions that fly under the radar, like siphoning money from the sale of a property or, as you rightly point out, putting their elderly parent into a less-than-ideal nursing home in order to preserve the lion's share of their inheritance.

Under the law, for a power of attorney to be valid, the principal must have been of sound mind and not under duress, restraint, fraud or undue influence. The National Center on Elder Abuse reports that research into elder abuse lags behind other areas of familiar and domestic abuse by as much as two decades.

Larceny, the theft of someone's property, is a felony in most states, depending on the amount stolen. Bad actors are betting on their family's legal inexperience and good nature to get away with their actions. There is a statute of limitations on elder financial abuse in most states, so it's important to act fast.

The Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association has a checklist for identifying financial abuse like withdrawing small amounts and changing POA or beneficiaries. Simfa recommends anyone with concerns contact the Eldercare Locator information specialist, available by calling 800-677-1116 on weekdays from 9 a.m. to 8 p.m. EST.

Related: My stepmother's lawyer called us 'greedy' for fighting for our father's estate. When will it end?

How to choose a good agent

If you have any doubt that a relative might be tempted to self-deal when overseeing your affairs - and remember that past actions are the best predictors of future actions - leave them out of your plans. Czepiga Daly Pope & Perri, a law firm with offices across Connecticut, warns its clients to choose wisely when setting up a POA.

"Powers of attorney are a wonderful tool in the hands of a trustworthy person," the law firm says. "But because it comes with a tremendous responsibility, it can also be a dangerous tool in the hands of the wrong person. We have had too many cases where the wrong choice resulted in exploitation or abuse by a family member or friend."

It adds: "Choose someone who is trustworthy and fair minded, understands his or her duties, and a commitment to taking those duties seriously, understands your wishes and your values, demonstrates loyalty to you. Never forget that you're giving your agent the opportunity to access your checking and savings accounts and other assets."

As you rightly point out, not choosing anyone to have power of attorney or not setting up an advance healthcare directive to inform your doctors what actions you want them to take (or not take) if you are unable to make those decisions yourself can leave a person in a vulnerable and unpleasant situation for months while the state appoints a guardian.

I hope others can learn from what you have witnessed.

Don't miss: I'm single, 75, and living with kidney disease and a pacemaker. I've no long-term care insurance. Will I be OK?

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

My friend's father gifted her Tesla and Google stock. They could be worth millions. Will they be split 50/50 in her divorce?

'I've been kept in poverty': Do I take a $70K job at 74 and lose my Section 8 housing allowance?

My aunt barely spends any money. Will we receive the contents of her safe-deposit box when she dies?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

08-23-25 0642ET

Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: 'The money went into his account': Is my father trying to swindle me out of my grandparents' $800K home?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I came across a letter from my father's bank showing a wire transfer of $195,000 from the buyer'

"Is it normal for part of the proceeds to be transferred directly to my father before the heirs receive their shares?" (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

My mother passed away while serving as the executor of her parents' estate. After her death, my father became the administrator and put their house on the market. We eventually found a buyer - our neighbor, who had known my father for many years. Over the years, this neighbor had expressed interest in purchasing the house whenever we were ready to sell.

I later learned that I am one of the heirs and will be entitled to 18% of the sale proceeds. The house was reported as sold for $800,000, but I came across a letter from my father's bank showing a wire transfer of $195,000 from the buyer, labeled "property purchase." The money went into his account! Is it normal for part of the proceeds to be transferred directly to my father before the heirs receive their shares?Should I be concerned about how the sale is being handled?

Puzzled in Delaware

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Don't miss: 'I can only rely on myself': How do I protect myself from my parents' nursing-home bills?

Your father should have put the money from the sale of this property into an account held in trust or an escrow account, and not into his personal bank account.

Dear Grandson,

Something is rotten in the state of Delaware.

Was that $195,000 meant as a down payment? Executors have a fiduciary duty to the heirs. That means they have a legal obligation to act in the best interest of the heirs, and not in their own best interest. They should act with care rather than recklessness and due diligence rather than impulsivity, avoid any conflict of interest, keep meticulous records of all financial and legal proceedings and keep the beneficiaries informed at all times. None of the above appears to have been done in this instance.

Executors may also be held personally liable for failing to carry out their duties in a fair, honest and efficient manner. At a minimum, they should act in good faith and with honesty and integrity. In this case, your father should follow the instructions of the will or trust, assuming there were instructions, as you say. He should also seek the best market price for this house, which does not involve selling it to the nearest person at a privately agreed-upon sum, which could leave room for bribery.

What's more, your father should have put the money from the sale of this property into an account held in trust or an escrow account, and not into his personal bank account. The reason for this is simple. This is not his money! It belongs to your grandparents' estate until it is dispersed to the heirs per the terms of the will or trust. Ask your father for detailed accounts related to the sale, and alert the attorney who was responsible for representing the estate in this transaction.

Using an estate account

"Opening an estate account is an important step when managing inherited property," says HomeLight, a real-estate technology company based in San Francisco. "This dedicated bank account will be used to handle all financial transactions related to the estate, including proceeds from the property sale. An estate account helps ensure transparency and keeps personal finances separate from estate funds, which can make it easier to track expenses and distribute proceeds among heirs."

"Before listing the property, it's important to assess its condition and current market value," HomeLight adds. "One option to determine the home's value is to contact an experienced real-estate agent who can provide a comparative market analysis. This is a detailed report that uses data from nearby recently sold homes to give you an accurate selling price." In addition, your father, as executor, should have hired a home appraiser and arranged a home inspection to make sure the house was valued correctly.

Current and fair market value

And now for the ugly truth: You may, if you don't get satisfaction, be faced with the uncomfortable decision of whether to take legal action against your father for a variety of potential or alleged misdeeds related to this house sale, which may go beyond depositing money into his own personal account. A less adversarial and less costly approach would be reaching a private resolution using a mediator once you have all the information in hand.

The bottom line for anyone considering executor duties: Being an executor is often a thankless job. The upside is that they have the right to pay themselves for the time and services they provide. But if they fail in their duties, they can be held liable for their actions. Some wills outline a fee, while some states recommend fees at 5% of the estate if it's valued at less than $100,000, 4% of the estate if it's between $100,000 and $200,000, and 3% if it's valued at between $200,000 and $1 million.

It's no picnic being an executor of an estate. The executor's responsibilities are extensive when dealing with the sale of a property - including maintenance of the real estate, paying the mortgage, making sure squatters don't occupy the property, clearing out and distributing furniture and other items to heirs, and staying current on insurance and real-estate taxes. Your father may have merely done the best he could under the circumstances, while making some major, if rookie, errors.

In this situation, nothing about this transaction appears normal.

Don't miss: I'm single, 75, and living with kidney disease and a pacemaker. I've no long-term care insurance. Will I be OK?

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

My friend's father gifted her Tesla and Google stock. They could be worth millions. Will they be split 50/50 in her divorce?

'I've been kept in poverty': Do I take a $70K job at 74 and lose my Section 8 housing allowance?

My aunt barely spends any money. Will we receive the contents of her safe-deposit box when she dies?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  08-23-25 0625ET
